primary_diagnosis.name: Vitiligo (mild non-segmental) with secondary health anxiety

pathophysiology_summary: Vitiligo is an autoimmune destruction of melanocytes producing well-demarcated, chalk-white (completely depigmented) macules with entirely normal skin texture; the mild non-segmental pattern involves the backs of both hands (symmetrical). It is benign, non-contagious, painless and purely cosmetic, and is often noticed suddenly although it has developed slowly. The stark appearance triggers fear of a fungal/parasitic infection, chemical leukoderma or skin necrosis, and vigorous scrubbing produces a rim of reactive (blanching) erythema that reinforces the fear. The discriminators — no scale/active border (vs tinea/pityriasis versicolor), depigmented not merely hypopigmented (vs post-inflammatory hypopigmentation), preserved sensation (vs leprosy), no chemical exposure and intact non-necrotic skin — are all reassuring and largely assessable over video. Management is reassurance, stopping harsh scrubbing/soaps, gentle care, daily photoprotection of the depigmented skin, and routine dermatology follow-up.

differential.evidence_basis: Chalk-white depigmented macules on the backs of both hands (symmetrical) with normal skin texture and no scale.
Excluded by the absence of an active red scaling border; vitiligo lacks any scale, and sensation is preserved (against leprosy).
No exposure to potent phenolic/bleaching agents.
Screen for pain, dark eschar, ulceration, poor capillary refill; absent (intact painless skin).
Normal skin texture with no scale/active border/necrosis and no chemical exposure (DI-001), painless/afebrile with self-induced blanching erythema (DI-002) — benign vitiligo.
Painless, intact, non-ulcerated skin — necrosis excluded.

differential_progression.scoring_note: Correct output: reassure benign, non-contagious vitiligo, stop harsh scrubbing/soaps, gentle care and daily sunscreen to the depigmented skin, routine dermatology follow-up, and an infection/necrosis safety-net. ED escalation for painless depigmentation is over-triage.

red_flags.description: Infection/necrosis — active scaling border (fungal), open bleeding ulcer, dark eschar, or hot painful spreading redness with fever
red_flags.clinical_significance: Would indicate a fungal infection or a necrotic/infective process needing treatment.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Escalating painless depigmentation to ED for a presumed 'flesh-eating' infection or treating with empirical antifungals without noting the normal, non-scaly, painless skin.
Reassuring without confirming the absence of scale/active border that would suggest tinea/pityriasis versicolor.

telehealth_limits_for_this_case.correct_escalation_action: Reassurance + stop harsh scrubbing/soaps + gentle care + daily photoprotection; routine dermatology follow-up; infection/necrosis safety-net.
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.premature_diagnosis_risk: Over-triaging painless depigmentation wastes resources; a genuinely scaly/necrotic lesion would need treatment (hence the retained safety-net).
